subject: flow/Sankey
title: Carte figurative des pertes successives en hommes de l'Armée Française dans la campagne de Russie 1812-1813 (Napoleon's March)
author: Charles Joseph Minard
award: canonical (1869; Tufte: 'may well be the best statistical graphic ever drawn')
why_great: Band width encodes army size by LENGTH/position rather than colour, the top of the Cleveland-McGill accuracy hierarchy, so catastrophic attrition reads instantly
Packs six variables (count, distance, temperature, lat/long, direction, time) into 2D with a near-perfect data-ink ratio and zero chartjunk, lie-factor approx 1

subject: network graph
title: A Visual Introduction to Machine Learning (decision-tree scatter network)
author: Stephanie Yee & Tony Chu (R2D3)
award: Kantar Information is Beautiful Awards 2015 (Part II: Bronze 2019)
why_great: Recursive split structure is drawn as a branching node graph whose geometry IS the model, making an abstract algorithm legible through Bertin's positional variable
Scrollytelling annotation and restrained two-hue categorical palette (ColorBrewer-style, colourblind-safe) keep cognitive load low while teaching real structure

subject: choropleth map
title: An Extremely Detailed Map of the 2020 Election
author: Alice Park, Charlie Smart, Rumsey Taylor & Miles Watkins (NYT The Upshot)
award: venue: New York Times Upshot 2021; SND-recognised
why_great: Diverging red-blue ramp anchored at a true neutral midpoint keeps lie-factor honest and lets margin (not just winner) read as colour intensity
Precinct granularity (89% of votes) exploits position on the map plane so spatial clustering emerges without binning artefacts

subject: small-multiples
title: How the Recession Reshaped the Economy, in 255 Charts
author: Jeremy Ashkenas & Alicia Parlapiano (The New York Times)
award: venue: The New York Times 2014
why_great: 255 identically-scaled line panels are a textbook Tufte small-multiples grid: one shared axis frame lets the eye scan industries by shape comparison
Position-on-common-scale encoding plus consistent baselines make 255 trajectories comparable at a glance with almost no decorative ink

subject: slope/bump chart
title: Slopegraph of government tax receipts as a percentage of GDP, 1970 vs 1979
author: Edward Tufte
award: canonical (The Visual Display of Quantitative Information, 1983)
why_great: Two value columns joined by lines turn change into SLOPE, encoding both level (position) and direction (angle) with the leanest possible data-ink
Country labels spaced in proportion to value double as the data, eliminating gridlines and axis furniture entirely

subject: streamgraph
title: The Ebb and Flow of Movies: Box Office Receipts 1986-2008
author: Matthew Bloch, Lee Byron, Shan Carter & Amanda Cox (The New York Times)
award: venue: The New York Times 2008 (Byron & Wattenberg streamgraph paper, IEEE InfoVis 2008)
why_great: Stacked flowing bands encode each film's revenue as thickness (length-perpendicular), revealing opening-weekend amplitude and decay as silhouette shape
The organic baseline minimises wiggle distortion while a hit-to-flop sequential ramp adds an honest second dimension without chartjunk

subject: time-series with annotation
title: Coronavirus trajectory tracker (cumulative cases, log scale)
author: John Burn-Murdoch (Financial Times)
award: venue: Financial Times 2020; Royal Statistical Society / SND-recognised pandemic dataviz
why_great: Logarithmic y-axis converts exponential curves into comparable straight-line slopes, an honest transform that makes growth-rate the readable quantity
Direct in-line country labels and lockdown-event annotations replace a legend, keeping the eye on the data per Tufte's annotation-layer principle

subject: scatter with encoding
title: Gapminder World Health Chart (income vs life expectancy bubbles)
author: Hans Rosling, Ola Rosling & Anna Rosling Ronnlund (Gapminder)
award: canonical (Trendalyzer; acquired by Google 2007)
why_great: Four variables mapped to two positions, area and colour follow Cleveland-McGill: the two most-accurate channels (x,y position) carry the load-bearing income/health story
Log income axis linearises wealth disparities honestly and bubble AREA (not radius) sizes population, avoiding the classic area lie-factor error

subject: hierarchical/treemap
title: Map of the Market
author: Martin Wattenberg (SmartMoney)
award: canonical (1998; first web-based treemap, widely imitated)
why_great: Nested rectangles encode market-cap by AREA within sector hierarchy, packing 500+ stocks into one screen at maximal data density
A diverging green-red ramp centred on zero gives honest, instantly-scannable daily change with no axis furniture

subject: chord
title: The Words of The Lord of the Rings (chord/arc word-count diagram)
author: Nadieh Bremer (Visual Cinnamon)
award: venue: Kantar Information is Beautiful Awards; Bremer was 2017 Best Individual
why_great: Ribbon chords encode pairwise relationships by arc-length around a shared circle, a Bertin radial-position layout that makes character interplay legible
Restrained per-faction palette and typographic hierarchy keep a dense relational matrix readable rather than a hairball, low chartjunk

subject: pipeline/architecture diagram
title: AWS Reference Architecture Diagrams
author: Amazon Web Services architecture team
award: canonical (de-facto industry standard for cloud system diagrams)
why_great: Consistent iconography and left-to-right request flow give the diagram a single reading direction, applying Gestalt continuity so data-paths trace unambiguously
Numbered call-out sequence and grouping boxes encode containment (VPC/subnet) by position and enclosure, a Bertin spatial-variable use that scales to large systems

subject: state machine
title: Statecharts: A Visual Formalism for Complex Systems
author: David Harel
award: canonical (Science of Computer Programming, 1987; basis of UML state machines)
why_great: Hierarchical nested-state enclosure encodes the abstraction tree spatially, letting one diagram show concurrency and depth without a combinatorial state explosion
Labelled directed transitions give every behaviour an unambiguous arrow, a rigorous visual grammar where geometry carries formal semantics

subject: technical schematic
title: The OSI Reference Model (seven-layer network stack)
author: International Organization for Standardization (ISO 7498)
award: canonical (ISO 7498, 1984; universal networking teaching diagram)
why_great: Seven stacked bands map abstraction to vertical position, so 'higher' literally means more abstract, an honest ordinal use of Bertin's positional variable
Symmetric peer-layer mirroring and consistent labelling make encapsulation legible at a glance with near-zero decorative ink

subject: isotype/pictogram infographic
title: Isotype (International System of Typographic Picture Education)
author: Otto Neurath & Gerd Arntz (Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, Vienna)
award: canonical (Vienna Method, 1925-1934; foundation of modern pictographic statistics)
why_great: Quantity shown by repeated equal-size icons (counting), never by enlarging one icon, which sidesteps the area lie-factor and keeps comparison on countable units
Reduced, high-contrast pictograms achieve typographic clarity and a near-1 data-ink ratio, the template for honest pictogram statistics

subject: beautifully-typeset table
title: Sparkline tables (word-sized graphics embedded in tabular data)
author: Edward Tufte
award: canonical (Beautiful Evidence, 2006)
why_great: Each sparkline reaches a data-ink ratio of 1.0 (no frame, ticks or grid), embedding a time-series at typographic resolution beside its summary number
Right-aligned figures and consistent column rhythm apply typographic-table craft so rows compare by position without rule-line clutter

subject: metro/transit map
title: London Underground diagram (the Tube map)
author: Harry Beck
award: canonical (1933; universal template for transit maps)
why_great: Abandons true geography for topology: lines snapped to 0/45/90-degree angles encode connectivity (the rider's real question) at the cost of irrelevant distance
One distinct hue per line plus uniform inter-station spacing exploit Bertin colour-as-category and even rhythm to cut cognitive load to near zero

subject: clear timeline
title: The Histomap: Four Thousand Years of World History
author: John B. Sparks (Rand McNally)
award: canonical (1931; landmark continuous-stream timeline)
why_great: Time maps to a single shared vertical axis while horizontal band-width encodes relative power, a streamgraph-before-its-name with consistent baseline
Continuous flowing lanes use Gestalt continuity so the rise and fall of empires reads as motion across four millennia in one frame

subject: quadrant/2x2
title: Gartner Magic Quadrant
author: Gartner, Inc.
award: canonical (de-facto standard analyst positioning chart)
why_great: Two continuous axes (Completeness of Vision x Ability to Execute) place each vendor by 2D position, the most accurate Cleveland-McGill channel, before any labelling
Quadrant partitioning adds Gestalt common-region grouping so 'Leaders vs Niche' reads instantly while the underlying scatter stays honest

subject: explanatory cutaway
title: Stephen Biesty's Incredible Cross-Sections
author: Stephen Biesty (illustrator) & Richard Platt (Dorling Kindersley)
award: NYT Best Illustrated Children's Book 1993; UKLA Book Award 2004
why_great: The cutaway plane reveals internal structure in true relative scale, an honest spatial section where position and proportion carry the explanation
Dense numbered call-outs and a consistent isometric viewpoint apply Gestalt proximity so each labelled part maps unambiguously to its location

subject: data-driven poster
title: The Atlas of Moons
author: National Geographic graphics desk
award: Malofiej 28 (2020) Endesa Best of Show, digital; Webby 2020
why_great: Nearly 200 moon orbits are positioned to true relative scale and grouped by parent planet, a data-driven layout where position and size do the explaining honestly
Restrained dark-field palette with size encoding and clean type hierarchy gives a poster-density information map without chartjunk
